X4.1.label: Question X4.1
X4.1.topic: Actuarial investigations, valuation, ALM modelling, and PMI persistency analysis.
X4.1.synopsis: Actuarial investigations, valuation of assets and liabilities, stochastic asset-liability modelling for investment strategy. Individual PMI persistency analysis, statistical modelling of shopping around behavior, and ethical considerations of selective premium discounts.
X4.1.scenario: A large, long-established, proprietary, composite insurance company transacts a wide range of long-term without-profits health insurance business.

(i).question: Outline the reasons for performing an actuarial investigation of the in-force business of the company.

Points: conform with legislative requirements (ie to publish)
calculate an embedded value so that profitability of in-force business can be monitored over time
calculate reserves and solvency capital and assess the real size of free assets
assess whether premiums or terms should be modified on reviewable business, and to what extent
modify the terms offered to new business (eg repricing or policy wording), by updating assumptions as to future experience, and by monitoring trends
help in the risk management of the business (eg to reconsider reinsurance, underwriting levels or claims control processes)
investigate dividend strategy and the use of capital
see what level of charges are required for unit-linked policies where the company has the discretion to alter their level
aid investment policy in order to maximise returns with acceptable risk
help control expenses, for example by identifying causes of any expense overrun
change the marketing message or sales procedures.

(ii).question:
The company is performing an internal valuation to establish the size of its free assets.

Describe briefly the possible approaches that could be used when establishing the size of the insurer's free assets.

Points: To answer this question adequately you must deal with how you might value each class of liabilities. You must also discuss the asset valuation.
Valuations carried out for internal purposes would usually use best estimate assumptions (rather than incorporating any margins).
**Conventional liabilities:** A realistic, prospective gross premium valuation, using a formula or cashflow approach.
Liabilities could be valued using an interest rate consistent with yields available on the assets...
... or alternatively, a risk-free discount rate could be used if a market-consistent valuation was required.
**Unit-linked liabilities:** The unit liability could be taken as the bid value of units.
Any non-unit liability could be assessed using a realistic cashflow approach.

**Mismatching reserve:** The company may wish to hold a realistic mismatching reserve if it is mismatched. Alternatively, it may ignore this in its best estimate scenario and consider it as part of any sensitivity analysis.
**Assets:** Possibilities are discounted value of future asset proceeds and market value.
Consistency between the asset and liability valuations is essential.
If the approach for the liabilities has been prospective, then assets could be valued by discounting expected future proceeds at a consistent interest rate.
Assets backing unit liabilities on unit-linked business would probably be taken at market value.

(iii).question:
The company has recently started using a stochastic asset model in its asset-liability modelling investigations to aid investment strategy.

Explain how asset-liability modelling may be used to determine an appropriate investment strategy for the company.

Points: The company will project its liabilities using a model office.
It will need to select suitable sample policies (model points) to represent the business.
It needs enough model points to make the model realistic, but few enough to keep computer run times to a reasonable length.
The liabilities are likely to be projected deterministically, although a stochastic inflation rate might be used for expenses.
The company will have to decide whether or not to include new business.
The model will be unrealistic without it, but it is difficult to predict.
The company will have to decide on a suitable number of simulations (eg 1,000 to 10,000)...
... and an appropriate time period (eg 5 to 25 years).
It will choose a trial investment distribution and project the returns on this investment mix using its stochastic model.
It can then vary the investment mix and repeat the exercise a number of times to see which mix is most appropriate.
To decide what is 'best', it needs to have chosen suitable criteria for judging this.
One possibility would be to maximise return subject to an acceptably low probability of insolvency.
An alternative criterion would be a high probability of maintaining sufficient free assets to allow investment freedom.

Another possible criterion would be maximising expected shareholder transfers subject to an acceptably low probability of an undesirable result (eg supervisory capital position too weak to attract new business).
The choice of parameters for the model, particularly the random terms, will have a large impact on the results, and so must be chosen with great care.
Dynamic interactions should be built into the model.

For example, lower investment returns should reduce projected benefits on pre-funded long-term care policies where the benefit depends on the fund at time of claim; declining supervisory free assets may cause a switch further into gilts.
Because of the complexity of the model, it will have to be 'solved' using simulation techniques rather than analytically.
All results should be checked for reasonableness.

(iv).question: Outline how the future investment income from the assets could be estimated.

Points: For all assets, make allowance for the expenses of investment.
For fixed-interest securities, the income and redemption proceeds are known in terms of dates and amounts.
An allowance should be made for any possibility of default (eg for non-government issues) and we normally ignore the possibility of earlier sale.
There is no difference for index-linked stocks, apart from the need to build in an estimate of future changes in the index.
For equities, the current dividend stream can be projected, taking into account the economic outlook and the progress of dividends generally.
Assumptions on equity market values will be needed for anticipated sales in the future, if any.
For property, rental income and capital growth can be projected, again taking into account the prospects for this asset class and the economy.
Conservative assumptions should be used for property, due to the lack of marketability (if they are to be sold).
Also, allowance could be made for particular aspects of properties, such as rental review terms, refurbishment costs and the risk of void.
Money-market investments can be valued as an infinite stream of income based on short-term interest rates.
Alternatively, if it is needed to pay imminent claims, it could simply be included as a lump sum income.
A mix of these two methods might also be suitable.
Overseas investments can be treated in the same way as domestic investments, with an allowance for exchange rates and the different risk of default.
Allowance should be made for the volatility of income and capital values of all the above assets.
The assumptions used for estimating the future asset income should be consistent with those used for estimating future liability cashflows.

(v).question:
A short-term insurance company, part of the same corporation, has been analysing its new business and renewal experience in its individual PMI business, which has been worsening over recent years. A director has suggested that policyholders who are more likely to shop around at renewal should be charged a lower premium in order to attempt to keep their custom.

State the reasons why an insurance company performs analyses of new business and renewal rates.

Points: looking at growth for different rating groups to monitor selection
to check the business mix against that assumed in the pricing basis
to check staffing levels against those needed for the business written
to check commissions paid against those assumed
getting an early indication of undue losses or gains
comparing sales growth against targets
checking new business strain against available capital
assessing the level of premium rates compared to competitors
assessing the impact of marketing initiatives, eg advertising spend
monitoring the trade-off between price and volume (ie assessing overall profitability by getting the balance of volume and profit per policy correct)
monitoring the level of accumulations of risk, eg to a regional epidemic

(vi).question: Suggest how you would assess a customer's likelihood of shopping around at renewal.

Points: Past data needs to be gathered on the characteristics of current and ex-policyholders, along with their persistency to date (ie how long they stayed with the company before they left, or how long they have been with the company so far).
For each policyholder, the characteristics will have been recorded for rating and administration purposes.
Some of these will clearly not have any great impact on likely persistency (eg smoker status) and may be ignored in order to keep the dataset manageable.
Some characteristics, such as mode of payment and age, will probably have a large impact on persistency and so might be analysed in more detail (eg by using small ranges of age).

A pattern then needs to be fitted to the observed persistency rates, so that given certain levels of each characteristic, a policyholder's persistency can be predicted with a reasonable degree of confidence.
This could be done using a variety of statistical techniques such as multivariate analysis techniques.

If possible, the premium difference on renewal (ie current premium less renewal premium) needs to be recorded too, so that the size of any change in premium can also be used as a parameter in the persistency formula.
The experience should be monitored once any revised premium rates are put in place, and the actual experience compared to that expected.
As a result of this, it may be necessary to change the formula used for calculating the expected renewal experience.

(vii).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the director's proposal.

Points: **Advantages:**
Should decrease costs such as marketing and new business administration, both for the company and the industry as a whole.
This in turn keeps industry premiums lower, which will please policyholders.
The customer has a choice and in theory is free to swap insurers, so the practice would be in line with a free market (but see below).
The premium will reflect more accurately the true cost to the company because expenses are lower on renewal than for a new policy.
This will go some way towards reducing cross-subsidies.
Those that are most likely to leave will be in good health, and therefore the risks the insurer will be keenest to retain.
**Disadvantages:**
However, those policyholders who have experienced poor health (and are therefore less likely to leave) will not have premiums reduced.
The idea may therefore be seen as penalising people in poor health, and hence unethical.
May not work, ie it may be impossible to predict persistency with any great certainty.
Goes against loyalty principle by rewarding those that are likely to leave.
Also, against principle of trust and customer protection
Costs to put the research / analysis and systems in place.
May lead to unfavourable press coverage for the company, for the actuarial profession or for the insurance industry generally.
May attract bad comments from regulators.
May attract further regulation from authorities, which in turn costs the industry money and would ultimately increase premiums industry-wide and restrict innovation and product development.

If overall premium levels are to be maintained, then higher premiums may be charged to other policyholders and new policyholders, thus reducing the likelihood of retaining or attracting those policyholders.
Any difference in premium will be very obvious if the insurer is operating a simple rating structure (eg only based on age).
However, if many factors are being used when rating, this discount may be less noticeable and thus more 'acceptable'.

X4.2.label: Question X4.2
X4.2.topic: Reinsurance benefits and alternatives for an IP insurer launching overseas.
X4.2.synopsis: Reinsurance benefits for Income Protection: Risk control, capital smoothing, capital provision, technical expertise in new territories, and alternatives to reinsurance merging/capital raising.
X4.2.scenario:
A non-life insurer specialising in individual income protection cover based in a developed country is considering selling its products in an overseas developing country. The insurer has asked a health reinsurance company to explain how reinsurers can assist in this venture.

Outline the points the reinsurer would make in its reply, including alternatives to reinsurance.

Points.question: Outline the points the reinsurer would make in its reply, including alternatives to reinsurance.

Points: **Benefits of reinsurance to the IP insurer:**
Reinsurance can help the insurer control its risks and smooth its profits in the face of potentially volatile business.
For example, stop loss reinsurance can protect the whole of an insurer's account from making significant losses, particularly in the initial years.

Proportional reinsurance can also allow the insurer to write more business by simply passing on a proportion of the risks to the reinsurer, thus making more efficient use of the insurer's limited capital.
Non-proportional reinsurance can also help protect the insurer from large claims.
For example, high earners claiming for a long time, or an unusually large number of claims from the one employer.
Each of the above may be particularly useful in this case, because the experience may be very uncertain and volatile, particularly until the business volumes build up.
Reinsurance might provide the capital required in order to finance the new venture.
However, the effectiveness of such arrangements may be reduced (or eliminated) under the local solvency regime (eg Solvency II).

Although it may be possible to construct an arrangement that allows credit to be taken in the regulatory balance sheet for future profits that are not otherwise allowed, eg under Solvency II for those arising beyond the contract boundary.
Reinsurance might improve the capital position in the following ways:
- the reinsurer pays initial commission to the insurer and in return receives a proportion of the future surpluses arising on the portfolio of business
- traditional reinsurance can be used to reduce risk-based capital requirements such as the Solvency II SCR.
Reinsurers can provide technical expertise and advice in a number of areas that are crucial to the launch of a new product.
This advice will be most useful where the reinsurer has experience in the particular territory and overseas market.
Reinsurers can help in establishing local distribution channels to sell the products.
They can help in establishing agreements and dealing with third parties, such as local underwriters, claims assessors and rehabilitation councillors.

Through negotiating with the local contacts established by the reinsurer, the insurer can gain a better understanding of the IP insurance markets, the attitude towards IP, the provision of State benefits and the culture of the territory.
Reinsurers may be able to provide access to local product information, eg policy conditions, proposal forms or claim forms.
They may be able to give information on competitors' rates to help in pricing.
This can help in designing products to suit the local market and in assessing the viability of the proposed launch. The reinsurer can provide additional assistance in both these matters.
Reinsurers can provide access to appropriate data to allow the insurer to produce accurate financial planning projections (to assess capital requirements) and for pricing.
They can provide advice on relevant legislation, regulations and taxation matters, including how these are expected to change in future.

Many of these benefits could be obtained directly, through acquiring, merging or setting up a joint venture with a local company. But, in these cases, reinsurers will be useful in setting up the arrangements in the first place.
Reinsurers can advise on, or are able to provide, new software systems. This may be appropriate for the company launching new products that are very different from its existing (domestic) ones.

Depending on how IP is taxed in the new market, there may be tax arbitrage on reinsurance arrangements. This is where the insurer and reinsurer are taxed on different bases so that the net result is better for both parties than before effecting the reinsurance.
The reinsurer may be able to provide other protection, eg specialist employer liability insurance, insurance against political or business risks, risk management services.
These above benefits would normally be obtained by the insurer passing on profits via reinsurance, rather than on a fee basis.
**Alternatives to reinsurance in achieving these objectives:**
Capital could be raised for the proposal from other sources. For example:
- investment markets
- existing shareholders or parent company
- the sale of parts of the business, subsidiaries or assets.
The insurer also may consider merging with another company (perhaps one in the target overseas territory).
There may in fact also be sufficient capital retained within the acquired company to fund the launch.
If they have the relevant experience, consultancies may be able to provide technical expertise, local knowledge and data.
**Conclusion:**
As the response is being written on behalf of the reinsurer, you may also complete your reply with a conclusion that favours the use of reinsurance, while remembering to remain objective.

X4.3.label: Question X4.3
X4.3.topic: Reinsurance strategy review for CI, LTC, IP, and new PMI market entry.
X4.3.synopsis: Reinsurance strategy review: Quota share, surplus, risk premium, excess of loss, and stop loss. Evaluating reinsurance needs for existing CI, LTC, IP products and new PMI market entry given a healthy solvency position.
X4.3.scenario:
A medium-sized proprietary long-term health insurance company has been selling critical illness, long-term care and income protection insurance for individuals in equal volumes for many years. The company is well established in the marketplace and, despite a recent spell of low premium rates, has been building up a healthy solvency position over the years. It is now considering a move into the private medical insurance market.
The chief executive wants to review the reinsurance arrangements appropriate for this company. They want to start with a clean slate, and ignore any current reinsurance arrangements.
They have heard of quota share, surplus, risk premium, excess of loss and stop loss reinsurance treaties, but want a report with a brief explanation of what these treaties are. More importantly, they want to know, with reasons, which of these types would and would not be recommended, and how each type should be used.
Describe the points that would be made in the report under the following headings:

Points: Quota share (QS) and surplus are proportional reinsurance arrangements, ...
... which means that an agreed (and known) proportion of each risk is ceded to the reinsurer.
For QS, the proportion is the same for all policies.
Under surplus the reinsurer pays claims above a fixed retention amount, and so the proportion of risk ceded will vary according to the size of benefit.
Both types can be written on an original terms (OT) basis, whereby premiums and claims on each policy would be shared between the cedant and the reinsurer in the same proportions.
Alternatively, they could be written on a risk premium (RP) basis, which usually only applies for long-term contracts (ie not PMI).
Under RP reinsurance:
- the reinsurer charges a specific premium for the reinsurance, which may be level over the term of the contract or vary according with the annual probability of claim
- the proportion of the risk ceded may be based on the sum insured or, alternatively, the sum at risk, which is the difference between the sum insured and the reserve held.
Excess of loss (XL) and stop loss (SL) treaties are non-proportional arrangements, ...
... whereby the reinsurer pays nothing until claims reach a certain level.
Under such arrangements, the reinsurer will pay for the cost of claims between an upper and lower limit.
This could be for each and every claim (Risk XL), for aggregation of claims (Aggregate XL or SL), or for catastrophes (Aggregate XL by event or Catastrophe XL).
The extent of an XL treaty is also defined in terms of reinstatement provisions, ie rules on how many times each layer of reinsurance cover can be used and the cost, if any, of allowing this.
A SL treaty may be defined using alternatives to fixed monetary limits, for example paying out once the loss ratio over a specified period exceeds a certain amount.

(ii).question: factors to consider when determining appropriate reinsurance arrangements

Points: Considerations when deciding on a suitable reinsurance programme include:
- the claims characteristics for each class, eg volatility
- the experience of the company in the marketplace
- the need for spreading risks
- the availability of reinsurance on suitable terms at suitable rates
- the security of reinsurers
- the size of the free assets
- alternatives to reinsurance
- the size of each class
- the degree to which it is felt that the business outcome is predictable within bounds
- the need for financial assistance
- the need for smooth dividends
- the need for technical assistance.
Given that the company is medium-sized and has a reasonably diverse product range, it will want to minimise the use of reinsurance to avoid ceding profit.
The company also has large free assets and so can withstand a significant amount of volatility itself - another reason for minimising the use of reinsurance.
A healthy solvency position suggests that arranging reinsurance to improve the capital position is unlikely to be necessary.
The prime use of the reinsurance will be to protect against volatile claims experience and to thus smooth profits / dividends for shareholders.
However, the new class of PMI will require significant use of reinsurance (see below).
The company may consider an experience refund (or profit share) arrangement, which will encourage it to manage the claims experience better, and so keep the cost of reinsurance down.

(iii)(a).question: how reinsurance could be used for the: existing critical illness insurance products

Points: The company has been selling this business for many years, which suggests it is unlikely to need significant reinsurance for this class.
On the other hand, claims experience may be limited because CI insurance is a relatively new product, claims are fairly infrequent and there are many different types of claim.
It is therefore unlikely that one company will have sufficient data at the level of detail required to be able to price with confidence.
Some reinsurance is therefore required to limit the insurer's exposure to adverse fluctuations in experience and to provide technical assistance.

For most types of CI policies, reserves are small, and so from the point of view of passing on a proportion of the full sum insured (under OT) or only the sum at risk (under RP), there is little difference between OT and RP.
The insurer may prefer OT so that the reinsurer is committed to the premium rates being charged.
Also, OT is administratively simpler and it may have possible tax advantages (as might level risk premium).
However, RP may be available more cheaply, particularly if the reinsurer can review the premiums.

As the company is likely to have a heterogeneous mix of business by benefit amount it probably only wishes to pass some of the larger risks over to the reinsurer, and so surplus would be more appropriate than QS.

Non-proportional arrangements might be considered (eg to smooth results), but are less likely to be required because the benefit amounts are known in advance (and so the experience is not likely to be very volatile). Also, these may not be available or affordable.

(iii)(b).question: how reinsurance could be used for the: existing long-term care insurance products

Points: Although this product has been sold for many years, the insurer may still lack a credible volume of claims data.
This product is also more complex than, for example CI insurance, suggesting that the need for reinsurance is greater than for CI insurance.
Reserves can be significant, and so if OT is used to share the risks, then deposit back arrangements may be preferred to avoid the reserve risk passing to the reinsurer.
RP would be a suitable alternative, and would target better the risks with large sums insured.
In addition, reinsurers may prefer RP, rather than being tied to the insurer's premium structure (under OT), which could be complex (eg with many different rating factors).
As for CI insurance, surplus may be preferred to QS to avoid ceding too much of the small risks, if the company has sufficient data to determine a suitable retention point.
However, the complexity of the product may result in QS being used because it is simpler.
On policies with indemnity benefits:
- surplus would not be appropriate because the proportion of risk ceded could not be determined in advance as the benefit amount is unknown
- non-proportional reinsurance might be considered.
On policies with fixed benefits, proportional reinsurance could be used, whereas non-proportional reinsurance might not be effective.

(iii)(c).question: how reinsurance could be used for the: existing income protection insurance products

Points: The company's experience and size on this established product suggests that its need for reinsurance might be minimal.
However, the uncertainty and volatility of experience suggests that some reinsurance may be desirable, particularly if premium rates are guaranteed.
As for CI insurance, either RP or OT may be used.
The reinsurer may prefer RP since premium rates can be complex (eg occupational loadings).
Surplus may be preferred over QS for the same reasons as given for CI insurance.
Again, non-proportional arrangements are less likely as per CI insurance and LTCI.

However, reinsurance that recovers (all or a proportion of) claims payments that are made following a specified duration of claim, would help protect the company from the risk of too many long-lasting claims.

(iii)(d).question: how reinsurance could be used for the: new private medical insurance products.

Points: The company will need extensive technical assistance for this venture into a new area, which could come from reinsurers.

This technical assistance will be needed for areas such as: product design, marketing strategy, access to data, access to market research, information on competitors, pricing, policy wording, underwriting, claims control / management, IT, technical training of staff.
As the company's own experience grows, it can lessen its reliance on reinsurance.
Claims are on an indemnity basis and so, depending on the benefit limits in the policies, large individual losses can occur.
For this reason, risk XL will be required.
However, a reasonably high retention can be used as the company is well-financed.

The upper limit does not need to be too large, given the financial position of the company and given that losses are potentially not too large (unless very expensive treatments were covered, for example).
There is also the risk of accumulations and so aggregate XL or SL might be considered to protect the company against the effect of many claims, eg from:
- a single event, such as an epidemic of infectious disease
- a type of treatment or illness, such as from an innovative new procedure (if covered).
This type of cover may not be available at a suitable price.
As the company is new in this area, it may also require QS in order to share the risks.
Surplus is not suitable because the claim amount is not known in advance and so the proportion of risk ceded cannot be pre-determined.
Financing arrangements - eg provided by QS in terms of commission - may not be effective under local solvency regulations (eg Solvency II), ...
... and are not important because the company is well-financed and the policies are short-term.

X4.4.label: Question X4.4
X4.4.topic: Factors to consider when deciding on reinsurance level for existing CI/LTC and new IP business in overseas markets.
X4.4.synopsis: Reinsurance considerations: Evaluating capital needs (regulatory, new business strain, investment freedom, adverse experience) and using reinsurance to manage insurance risk, reduce capital strain, and obtain technical assistance for new products.
X4.4.scenario: A small health insurer offers critical illness insurance and long-term care insurance. The company wishes to expand outside its domestic market to sell income protection insurance in a number of other developed economies. It has been proposed that the company reinsure some of the mortality, morbidity and longevity risks.

Factors.question: Discuss the factors the company should consider in deciding on an appropriate level of reinsurance for both the existing business and the new business.

Factors: **(i) Why the company needs capital**
*To meet regulatory requirements:*
The company must ensure it has sufficient capital to meet its policyholder liabilities as they fall due, under a wide range of possible scenarios.
A risk margin plus at least the MCR must be held for all classes of business.
The company will want to hold enough capital to comfortably cover the SCR to avoid regulatory intervention.
*To write new business (including attracting new business):*
Writing new business normally results in a capital strain, since the sum of the technical provisions and solvency capital is usually more prudent than the pricing basis.
We would expect that the best estimate liability would be negative for its new business if it is written on a profitable basis.
Once the risk margin and SCR are added to the best estimate liability, we would expect a capital strain.

Holding sufficient capital to avoid regulatory intervention is especially important for this company as its sole distribution is through financial advisers, who attach great importance to a company's financial strength.
Although the risk margin and SCR will be released over time, this might well be insufficient to support the planned levels of new business growth.
*To maintain investment freedom:*
The main investment principles are to:
- match assets to liabilities
- invest to maximise overall return (income and capital).
The extent to which the first may be departed from to pursue the second depends on the level of capital available and the desired level of risk.
More capital is needed to cover the SCR if a risky investment strategy is chosen.
So, more capital means a riskier investment strategy may be pursued, resulting in higher expected returns.
*To protect against adverse experience:*
A higher level of free capital will afford the company greater protection against all kinds of adverse experience.
This will reduce the need for reinsurance of morbidity and longevity risks ...
... and also help absorb the impact in other areas, such as expense overruns.
*To support strategic objectives:*
The company would require capital if it wishes to acquire:
- another insurer
- or develop a new distribution channel (eg a tied agent)
- a financial services company (eg a bank).
*To provide internal working capital:*
The company would require capital to support:
- product development costs (eg for the new income protection insurance), such as systems changes and marketing
- temporary special offers
- absorption of exceptional costs (eg of compliance reviews) that the company does not want to pass on to policyholders for competitive reasons.
**(ii) Use of reinsurance**
*Managing insurance risk (existing and new business):*
The company might seek reinsurance for its long-term care portfolio, if it is worried about the longevity risk.
Reinsurers might be willing to take on this risk if they have not already written a significant amount of annuity reinsurance, as it would provide diversification from any mortality risk.
However, longevity risk can't be diversified away by taking on more and more portfolios, so there will be limited capacity in the market. Since this insurer is small, this might not be an issue.

It might seek reinsurance for its critical illness insurance portfolio, if it is worried about the impact of individual large claims, or an accumulation of a large number of claims, on its solvency position.
Original terms (OT) reinsurance involves a sharing of all risks between the direct writer and the reinsurer.

Risk premium (RP) reinsurance is often based on the sum at risk and so would specifically target the morbidity risk. This would therefore usually be the more suitable arrangement for protection products, unless reserves are small.
Either of these arrangements could be used to reduce the morbidity risk on the critical illness insurance policies, original terms possibly being more suitable, given the low reserves.
Reinsurance can be proportional (the risk passed to the reinsurer is specified as a percentage of claims) or non-proportional (the reinsurer pays the excess over a given retention limit).
Proportional reinsurance is useful to limit the exposure whilst building up credible experience for a new product. Non-proportional is useful to reduce volatility of claims experience.
This is especially important due to the company's weak capital position. For this reason a fairly low retention might be chosen.

Catastrophe XL cover will be wanted to protect against large losses from a single event on the critical illness insurance portfolio, especially if there is any group business. However, the cost of this cover can be high, reducing its practicality.
Reinsurance can be used to reduce risk-based capital requirements, such as the Solvency II SCR ...
... although there will be some offset to the reduced insurance component due to the higher counterparty risk exposure.
Overall, the use of traditional reinsurance will improve the capital position as a result of reducing risk within the portfolio.
*Direct capital management:*
The company also needs to consider the extent to which reinsurance can be used to reduce new business strain.
Financing commission is available with both OT and RP reinsurance. This would help improve the capital position.

In the past, financial reinsurance arrangements have been used in some jurisdictions to improve the regulatory balance sheet by crystallising the value of future expected profits. However, the effectiveness of such arrangements is much reduced (or eliminated) under Solvency II or under an economic capital balance sheet.
*New product:*
If treaties are entered into, then reinsurers are likely to be willing to provide technical assistance.
This will be useful for pricing the new income protection insurance, since the company won't have any relevant past experience on which to base its calculations.
The reinsurer should also be able to help with product design.
Also, claims volatility is likely to be high initially on a small volume of business.
Other possible areas of assistance with the income protection insurance include:
- helping establish the underwriting process
- systems design

It is unlikely that all new business would fall within the terms of a reinsurance treaty, so facultative reinsurance might be used for some cases (eg very large sums assured or cases acceptable only on non-standard terms).
*General consideration:*
All external reinsurance comes at an expected cost, so this should be weighed against the benefits before proceeding.
